words.shortDefinition: A lightweight, standalone, executable package of software.
words.longDefinition: A standard unit of software that packages up code and all its dependencies so the application runs quickly and reliably from one computing environment to another. Unlike VMs, containers share the host's OS kernel.
words.techContext: The fundamental unit of modern DevOps, allowing developers to say 'it works on my machine' and know it will work in production.
words.exampleSentence: We spun up a new container for the worker process to handle the sudden spike in background tasks.

words.shortDefinition: A read-only template used to create containers.
words.longDefinition: A Docker image is a set of instructions for creating a Docker container. It includes the application code, libraries, tools, and other dependencies. It is built using a Dockerfile.
words.techContext: You can think of an image as a class and a container as an instance of that class.
words.exampleSentence: I pulled the official Postgres image from Docker Hub to use as the base for our database container.

codeExample.code:
# Pull an image
docker pull node:20-alpine

# List local images
docker images

words.shortDefinition: A text document that contains all the commands a user could call on the command line to assemble an image.
words.longDefinition: A script used to automate the creation of Docker images. It defines the base image, environment variables, files to copy, and the command to run when the container starts.
words.techContext: The 'recipe' for your environment. It should be kept in version control alongside your code.
words.exampleSentence: I updated the Dockerfile to include the latest security patches for our Node.js runtime.

codeExample.code:
FROM node:20
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm install
COPY . .
CMD ["npm", "start"]

words.shortDefinition: The intermediate images created during the build process.
words.longDefinition: Each instruction in a Dockerfile creates a new layer in the image. Layers are cached to speed up subsequent builds. Only the changes between layers are stored, making images very space-efficient.
words.techContext: Understanding layers is key to optimizing build times. Put instructions that change frequently (like COPY .) at the end of the Dockerfile.
words.exampleSentence: By reordering our Dockerfile, we ensured that the 'npm install' layer is cached unless the package.json file actually changes.
words.etymology: from Middle English leyer ('that which lies').

words.shortDefinition: A mechanism for persisting data generated by and used by Docker containers.
words.longDefinition: Since container file systems are ephemeral (data is lost when the container is deleted), volumes are used to store data on the host machine or in external storage. They are managed by Docker.
words.techContext: Essential for databases. You mount a volume to /var/lib/postgresql/data so your data survives a container restart.
words.exampleSentence: We mapped a Docker volume to the uploads directory so that user-uploaded images wouldn't disappear when we redeployed the app.

codeExample.code:
# Run a container with a volume
docker run -v my-data:/app/data my-app

words.shortDefinition: A storage and content delivery system for named Docker images.
words.longDefinition: A service that hosts Docker images. You 'push' images to a registry to share them and 'pull' them to deploy them. Examples include Docker Hub, Amazon ECR, and Google Container Registry (GCR).
words.techContext: Our CI/CD pipeline automatically builds a new image and pushes it to our private Azure Container Registry after every successful merge.
words.exampleSentence: The deployment failed because the production server didn't have the correct credentials to pull the image from our private registry.

words.shortDefinition: The main command that is executed when a container starts.
words.longDefinition: An instruction in a Dockerfile that allows you to configure a container that will run as an executable. Unlike CMD, the ENTRYPOINT command is not easily overridden when the container is run.
words.techContext: Often used for initialization scripts that set up a database or environment before starting the main application process.
words.exampleSentence: We used an entrypoint script to run database migrations before starting the web server.

words.shortDefinition: A way to mount a file or directory from the host machine into a container.
words.longDefinition: Unlike volumes (which are managed by Docker), bind mounts can be any file or folder on the host system. They are often used in development to sync source code from the host into the container in real-time.
words.techContext: I used a bind mount to map my local 'src' folder into the container so I could see my code changes immediately without rebuilding the image.
words.exampleSentence: Bind mounts are great for development, but for production data, Docker volumes are generally preferred for better performance and management.

words.shortDefinition: A tool for defining and running multi-container Docker applications.
words.longDefinition: Uses a YAML file to configure your application's services, networks, and volumes. With a single command (docker-compose up), you can create and start all the services from your configuration.
words.techContext: The standard way to manage a local development environment consisting of a web app, a database, and a cache.
words.exampleSentence: I added a Redis service to our docker-compose.yml so we can test the new caching logic locally.

codeExample.code:
version: '3.8'
services:
  web:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "3000:3000"
  db:
    image: postgres

words.shortDefinition: The background process that manages Docker objects.
words.longDefinition: Also known as 'dockerd'. It listens for Docker API requests and manages images, containers, networks, and volumes. It can also communicate with other daemons to manage Docker services.
words.techContext: When you run 'docker build', your CLI client is actually sending a request to the Docker daemon running on your machine.
words.exampleSentence: I had to restart the Docker daemon because it was having trouble communicating with the container runtime.
words.etymology: from Greek daimōn ('spirit, guardian'), used in computing for background processes.

words.shortDefinition: Mapping a port on the host machine to a port inside the container.
words.longDefinition: Allows external traffic to reach a service running inside a container. Since containers have their own isolated network, you must explicitly define which ports are accessible from the outside.
words.techContext: We mapped host port 80 to container port 3000 so that we could access our Node app via 'localhost' in the browser.
words.exampleSentence: I checked the port mapping because I couldn't connect to the database container on the default port.

codeExample.code:
# Map host port 8080 to container port 80
docker run -p 8080:80 nginx

words.shortDefinition: A network that enables communication between Docker daemons on different hosts.
words.longDefinition: Creates a distributed network among multiple Docker daemon hosts. This network sits on top of (overlays) the host-specific networks, allowing containers connected to it to communicate securely regardless of which host they are on.
words.techContext: Essential for Docker Swarm and Kubernetes, where containers are spread across a cluster of servers.
words.exampleSentence: The microservices communicate over an overlay network, so they don't need to know the physical IP addresses of the servers they are running on.

words.shortDefinition: A Dockerfile technique that uses multiple FROM statements to optimize image size.
words.longDefinition: Allows you to use one image for building (with compilers/tools) and a much smaller image for the final production runtime. You copy only the compiled artifacts from the first stage to the second.
words.techContext: We used a multi-stage build to compile our Go app in a 500MB image, then copied the binary into a 5MB alpine image for production.
words.exampleSentence: Switching to multi-stage builds reduced our final image size from 1.2GB down to just 80MB.

codeExample.code:
# Stage 1: Build
FROM node AS builder
RUN npm run build

# Stage 2: Serve
FROM nginx:alpine
COPY --from=builder /app/dist /usr/share/nginx/html

words.shortDefinition: The starting point for a Docker image build.
words.longDefinition: Specified by the FROM instruction in a Dockerfile. It provides the initial filesystem and OS tools that your application will build upon. Popular base images include 'alpine', 'ubuntu', and language-specific images like 'python' or 'node'.
words.techContext: Choosing a small, secure base image like 'alpine' is a best practice for container security and performance.
words.exampleSentence: We changed our base image to a minimal Debian-slim to fix some compatibility issues we had with Alpine's musl library.

words.shortDefinition: A lightweight Linux distribution commonly used as a Docker base image.
words.longDefinition: Based on musl libc and busybox. It is much smaller than traditional distributions (around 5MB), making it ideal for creating tiny, fast-loading Docker images. However, it can sometimes have compatibility issues with binary packages.
words.techContext: Most of our microservices use the alpine tag to keep our container registry usage low and deployments fast.
words.exampleSentence: Using the Node-Alpine image helped us cut our cold-start times in half because the image layer downloads so quickly.
words.etymology: Named after the Alps mountains, implying something small and clean.

words.shortDefinition: Images that contain only your application and its runtime dependencies.
words.longDefinition: They do not contain package managers, shells, or any other programs you would expect to find in a standard Linux distribution. This significantly reduces the attack surface and image size.
words.techContext: For our most critical security services, we use Google's distroless images to ensure that even if an attacker gets in, they don't even have a shell to run commands.
words.exampleSentence: We moved our production API to a distroless image to eliminate vulnerabilities related to unused OS utilities like curl or bash.

words.shortDefinition: A Linux kernel feature that limits and isolates resource usage of containers.
words.longDefinition: Also known as cgroups. They allow the host OS to set limits on CPU, memory, disk I/O, and network usage for a group of processes (the container). This prevents one container from hogging all the host's resources.
words.techContext: When you run a container with '--memory=512m', Docker is actually using cgroups under the hood to enforce that limit.
words.exampleSentence: The container was killed by the OOM (Out Of Memory) killer because it exceeded the limit set by its control group.

words.shortDefinition: A Linux kernel feature that provides process isolation.
words.longDefinition: Namespaces ensure that a process in one container cannot see or affect processes in another container or the host. Types include PID (process IDs), NET (networking), and MNT (file system mount points).
words.techContext: Namespaces are the reason why 'process 1' inside a container is not the same as 'process 1' on the host machine.
words.exampleSentence: Docker uses network namespaces to give each container its own virtual network stack and IP address.

words.shortDefinition: The world's largest library and community for container images.
words.longDefinition: The default public registry for Docker. It hosts official images for almost every major software project (Redis, MySQL, Node, etc.) as well as millions of community-contributed images.
words.techContext: I searched Docker Hub to find a pre-configured image for our CI testing environment.
words.exampleSentence: We use Docker Hub to pull our base images, but we host our proprietary code images in our own private registry.

words.shortDefinition: A way to label different versions of a Docker image.
words.longDefinition: Tags are aliases for image IDs. Common tags include 'latest', version numbers (e.g., '1.2.3'), or environment names (e.g., 'prod'). Proper tagging is essential for version control and rollback strategies.
words.techContext: Never use the 'latest' tag in production; always use a specific version tag to ensure your environment is reproducible.
words.exampleSentence: I tagged the new build as 'v2.1.0-beta' so the QA team could pull and test it specifically.

codeExample.code:
# Tag an image
docker tag my-app:latest my-registry.com/my-app:v1.0.1

words.shortDefinition: Docker's native container orchestration tool.
words.longDefinition: A tool that allows you to manage a cluster of Docker daemons as a single virtual system. It handles service discovery, load balancing, and scaling across multiple nodes.
words.techContext: While Kubernetes is more powerful, Docker Swarm is often preferred for smaller projects because it is much simpler to set up and manage.
words.exampleSentence: We used Docker Swarm to deploy our application across three servers for high availability.

words.shortDefinition: An industry-standard container runtime.
words.longDefinition: A CNCF (Cloud Native Computing Foundation) graduated project that manages the complete container lifecycle on its host system, from image transfer/storage to container execution and supervision.
words.techContext: Docker itself now uses containerd under the hood to handle the actual running of containers.
words.exampleSentence: Since Kubernetes deprecated Docker-shim, we now use containerd directly as our container runtime on all our nodes.

words.shortDefinition: The default command and/or parameters for an executing container.
words.longDefinition: An instruction in a Dockerfile that provides defaults for an executing container. Unlike ENTRYPOINT, the CMD instruction can be easily overridden by arguments passed to 'docker run'.
words.techContext: If you provide an ENTRYPOINT, CMD becomes the default arguments for that entrypoint.
words.exampleSentence: I used CMD to set the default log level for our app, which developers can override if they need more verbose output.

codeExample.code:
# Default command
CMD ["npm", "start"]

# Or arguments for ENTRYPOINT
ENTRYPOINT ["node"]
CMD ["app.js"]

words.shortDefinition: The preferred storage driver for modern Docker installations.
words.longDefinition: It is a union filesystem that allows Docker to create the layered image structure. It is highly performant and stable compared to older drivers like 'aufs' or 'devicemapper'.
words.techContext: We had to upgrade our kernel to ensure we could use the overlay2 driver for better disk I/O performance.

words.shortDefinition: Lasting for a very short time; the nature of container storage.
words.longDefinition: In the context of containers, it means that any data written to the container's own writable layer is lost once the container is removed. Permanent data must be stored in volumes.
words.techContext: Containers should be treated as ephemeral; you should be able to kill and replace any container at any time without losing critical state.
words.exampleSentence: Since the local container storage is ephemeral, we store all application logs in an external logging service.
words.etymology: from Greek ephēmeros ('lasting only a day').
